item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The piece is well written and it took me two reads, spaced by more than a year, to reach a conclusion, but ultimately it was a downvote.

Several aspects helped in this decision, but most boil down to the pathos of the piece being predicated on the Christianity of all the involved, including the reader. On the last tab it works, the drama of the character marries well with the piece, but on the rest of the article?

If it were write in such a way to convey the horror of someone loosing their own faith regardless, or if the punch were on the potential consequences of such a crude rewrite on history, maybe that would be another thing, but for me this is too much based on being scary for Christians for theological reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh believe me. She realizes and even says herself that if the cicada did eat something there that it could never have been the real god she had always worshipped. Believe me I am familiar with the capital g concept. I subscribe to the concept that if something isn’t infinite in every way, it’s not god. And so does she. But that doesn’t mean it doesn’t break her heart. This entity proves to her that either the god she worshipped never existed as she thought or that it just does not care and allow this thing to do its wild moves in gods name. She’s going through a lot!

That’s where part of the terror comes from me. The thought that god wasn’t what you thought or something else entirely was hearing your prayers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is obviously impossible to be a believer in Roman Catholicism and to believe that the Foundation can use their anomalous artifacts to erase Christianity, or to believe that the 3004 entity threatens God's creation. The God of Roman Catholicism - and most modern Christian sects - is the big boy Capital G God. Creator of Heaven and Earth and all that jazz. If it can be subsumed and defeated by an extradimensional paranormal entity, then it's obviously not all it's cracked up to be. If the Foundation's power is capable of retroactively erasing knowledge of Christ's sacrifice, then the principles of the Roman Catholic Church are nonsense.

But this isn't a problem at all. It's an absolutely great bit of characterization for the Ethics Committee member Bridget Callaghan. Callaghan has no problem asserting that the Foundation has the capability to undo the sacrifice of Jesus Christ, and consequently setting up the Foundation as a power superior to God. Moreover, they approved of such a tactic, which means that on some level they believe that not only can the Foundation defeat the power of Jesus Christ, but that does so is necessary.

The horrors they've had to work with in their time at the Foundation have completely short-circuited their moral logic. That the Ethics Committee has a Christian who thinks they ought to use thaumaturgy to countermand God is a devastating indictment of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept of this is incredible, it really is. I was hooked from early on when I realized what you were doing, and I got to say that you nailed the execution. The description of the rituals is terrifying, and the imagery stuck in my mind for quite a bit as well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Far and away one of my favorite articles on the site.

SIGHTSEE-LUX gets me every time — I don't know why, but this is maybe the best portrayal I've ever read of an unthinkable deific abomination. All the imagery is just so vivid, and haunting, and engrossing at the same time. Thoroughly chilling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not exactly a big fan of SCPs/skips/articles that go so much beyond the limits of our history, mostly because I like to think of the Foundation as capable of keeping this reality for some other centuries, not break down every day or so.

You know, we have 2000, which is basically a huge restart button, but that one keeps history intact, it doesn't make big changes. It's just a restart button.

Said so, this is one of such skips that I actually like even given this prejudice of mine.
So cheers, and have my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It appears scp-343 has to put a deity in its place

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of things I really liked about this one.

The gradual realization that the Catholic Church's attempts to destroy the Cétlaidí religion through assimilating its culture ultimately backfired by allowing it to survive unseen and and providing it with a more plentiful source of power and sustenance was very interesting. The idea that this entity of abstract belief transcended its previous limitations by incorporating into itself the very concept of monotheism, even more so.

I had found the tooth-pulling imagery in 2852 somewhat arbitrary, so the explanation that it originated from religious rites of passage associated with children losing their baby teeth really tied it together for me.

The notion that the Foundation might become so desperate to contain something that they resort to erasing one of the world's largest religions is very intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
per kinch's comment from 2018:

I do want to say that the Jude dying here very much isn’t our Jude. I like to keep GAW in a happier place than my cicada god stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Catholic reality warper in a kinchtheknifeblade skip, I would expect him to be Jude "bluntfiend" Kriyot of Gamers Against Weed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if St. Jude is J. Clark, S.J. from the archived report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ST JUDE NOOOO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is God ever gonna be an SCP? Not like, the old guy in the clouds. The actual lovecraftian nightmare accurate to the Bible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep thinking about this one years after I first read it. As a Christian, it scares the living bejesus out of me and remains one of the most terrifying things on the site for me. You did a fine job here, Kinch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted. Maybe it's that I'm biased being from Ireland, but the idea that the veneration of the supposed benevolent God could actually be fueling something far from benevolent is chilling. Love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the formatting got an update. It looks nicer now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it got a new haircut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think kinch might've changed the images? I don't know, looks practically the same to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmmm
something is different
cant quite decide what

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably those SCPs take place before DaB is activated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Came back to this one for a re-read and it left me as speechless as the last time I read it. Good fuck I love this. Firmly one of my personal top 3 from the 3000 contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering this “demiurge” is nothing more than a cosmic parasite that has deluded itself into believing it is the very god that it devoured I’d say yes. Especially since the alternative is letting this thing break into the material plane and devoure all that is left of god, his creation. At least if they destroy this thing everything god did before his destruction will have some meaning, as his children will continue his legacy, rather than letting this THING become the only thing there is, was, and ever will be

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this scip up until the very end of the last tap. It seemed to me that they were definitely executing DaB; if the did so, so many SCPs wouldn't exist - most importantly this one. They very clearly didn't go through with DaB, so why make the ending note for the scip them doing so?

I would much rather have been left with the idea of the suffering saint waiting in the apparatus for the day his faith ceases to exist entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s hard to not make destroy God feel cheap, and you managed to do it. As a Catholic this is chilling and as a piece of fiction this is both well-written and terrifying, specially the descriptions of what actually meeting a potestade would feel like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the idea of the last and greatest Christian saint being the one to undo and effectively destroy Christianity. A religious person whose god has been devoured sacrificing the one way of worship they believe to be true only to avenge the figure they thought to be all powerful is a strong theme. Is destroying the teachings and commandments of a dead god, to slay what is basically the demiurge, the correct thing to do when looked through a religious lens?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The content of the last tab is really beautiful, tragic, and heroic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do want to say that the Jude dying here very much isn’t our Jude. I like to keep GAW in a happier place than my cicada god stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I came upon a child of God, he was walking along the road …

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I bloody love this scip.

Is this by any chance inspired by Disney's Jiminy Cricket? His name is a pun for Jesus Christ, he's the conscience that bugs people mind, and SCP 3004 is a cicada on the cross.

I might think too deeply about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea in the last tab of gaining some measure of satisfaction out of killing that which devoured God. Even if God wasn't quite what the author thought. It was OUR god, damn it.

Overall, I think it's my favourite SCP of the hundreds I've read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta say, I really like this one. The tone is unusual for this site, though not in a bad way, and the eerie feeling the page evokes is great, as well as the hopelessness of the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to have to agree with mlister here. I'm novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to have to reverse my vote with this update. It's more of an ending, but… the pathos feels cheap. The destruction of something familiar (Jude and Christianity) for the explicit purpose of forcing the audience to feel a certain way, rather that letting it happen more organically. Besides that, the prose feels very hammy and overdramatic, which doesn't help with the former point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The format is susceptible to being bent or broken to best fit the story. The idea that any given SCP article takes place in a reality that, atleast superficially, is ours is part of that format. Simply put, the article had a story to tell, and it didn't let a common trope or assumption get in the way of telling that story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilerish question for the class
 

The last tab makes it sound like the Protocol has been/is about to be executed. Since Christianity still exists in the real world, is this meant to…

  1. Open an alternate universe/canon which might be explored further
  2. Suggest that this happened and the religion we know as Christianity, despite being similar to what is described here, is not in fact the actual Christianity?
  3. Imply that they failed and the Bug God is still out there?
  4. Suggest that any day now the world will be rewritten and Christianity will cease to exist?

…Or did I misunderstand something along the way? I'm often confused when pages seem to conflict with the idea that the Foundation exists in the world as we know it - I know this is fiction, but I'm still kinda under the impression that that is one of the central premises of the site. Novote for now, from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GHHHHH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I agree with some of the critique that this piece needed a description of Protocol Damnatio as Bestias, so I thought the majority decision from the Ethics Committee could be a good vehicle for this. The easter egg here is hardly an easter egg and may be very obvious, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fourth tab was very good. Up until the report, this felt way too similar to 2852 without much new information about the underlying cause, but the report really drove it home. I also wish we got more detail on what exactly 'Protocol Damnatio ad Bestias' entails, but that's a minor quibble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the most terrifying of the 3000 contest entries I've read, personally: not because of the cicadas (insects are very cool) but because I find religion very scary. Plus the idea of so totally erasing a huge chunk of Irish culture. Which Christianity kinda did in real life, anyway. Brr!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A decent continuation to one of my favorites articles? Sure, I'll take it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sentence is grammatically correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is as interesting as it is creepy.

… Just take your upvote. Take it and leave me alone, we don't get cicadas up here in Canada.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Blasphemy! It's a giant cicada shaped blasphemy!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apologies if I'm misinterpreting this, but doesn't the claim that 2852 events ceased after the first 3004 event directly conflict with the image and such on 2852? Or does it mean the first Foundation witnessed 3004 event?

Also, is there a word missing here after "primarily"?

Despite appearing in every way alive, SCP-3004-2 instances are primarily of wood and glass

Well done.

I quite enjoyed this, the last section pulled it together well and gave me a nice creepy "Oh shit" moment. One I really am going to think about later on, which is always a great thing for an article for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt alright about this one most of the way through, but the last log sort of fell flat for me. Like it was a little unprofessional? I'm not sure how to describe it. It's not bad now, just something didn't quite work right.

-1, but I'll probably come back and read this one again and revise that.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent change to tags was reverted, as it introduced an incorrect tag ("bug" is not a tag used by the site).

Community members, if you are not familiar with tags, please do not add new tags yourself. We have a team of experienced taggers that handles the addition of new tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy！Holy！Holy！Holy the Cicada！

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one was weird to read. Up until the last tab, it was really well written and executed (and some really good details here and there, especially in the 'current version'), but still nothing much more than by-now-boring tropes I've seen before far too many times.

Then the last tab took it to another level. The thing is, I expected the last tab to attempt that, and was resistant to it. But it still worked on me. It helps that the general execution is excellent. And the idea of it hitting the snooze button, its perspective and attitude, being 'all there is'…

The problems with the article are still there, but it won me over. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They probably are thinking monastic scapulars which are more like tabards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What wild fucking scapulars do you have?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A devotional scapular consists of two small squares liked by ribbons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No way you could get enough ball sacks to make a scapular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Image is sourced from here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Adriatic_Cicada_02.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a more than adequate sequel to SCP-2852, and I liked it enough to upvote. I don't think that is as good overall as the best contest entries though. In particular I had the same problem with the second tab that psul does, rather than coming across as timeless it just feels anachronistic.

. I'm a member of Mobile Task Force Sigma-25. We've always called ourselves “Ghostbusters.”

Doesn't that cause some confusion with Mu-13? You'd think the Foundation would encourage unique nicknames.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this is a lot like a villain I ran in a Pathfinder game (though scaled way up). Of course THERE, my players could all go to the Astral plane and whack it with swords until it died. They had a hell of a time figuring out what was going on though.

Anyway, I like this one. Cicadas, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it needs belief to exist, I think the intention is just that something about the Cetlaidi resonated enough with it for it to take notice of us. Like hearing something about yourself on the news. When the Cetlaidi were destroyed, the show ended and it went back to bed, but NOW he knows what channel we're on, and he can tune back in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that definitely feels a little weird. People converted en masse to another religion-type thingy because they thought their own religion-type thingy that they grew up with was too unpleasant rather than them just not believing it anymore? Cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, as this notes the line between pagan and Christian is a lot hazier in Celtic regions: a lot of people converted more because the fae were generally jerks than that they stopped believing in them.1 Appeasement offerings/shrines to the tommyknockers are documented well into the early 1900's, for example, and miners still only mostly kid when they attribute mishaps and weird noises to them.

Footnotes
1. I freely admit that even as a third generation Welsh-Irish American I have a little voice in the back of my head telling me I'm asking to end up in a horror movie when I call the Fair Folk jerks. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, that last collapsible was really great.
